data.title: MongoDB's Growth Engine Stalls: Atlas Deceleration Signals Deeper Problems Ahead (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

MongoDB’s (NASDAQ:MDB) stock is down 34% since my Buy rating in November. The NoSQL database leader was seeing robust growth (27%) from MongoDB Atlas (a database-as-a-service) at that time, but faced notable competition from Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and Google (GOOGL). A challenge of MongoDB’s was that despite being in business for nearly two decades, it has yet to achieve consistent free cash flow (FCF), thanks to large S&M and R&D investments. I noted that these investments were necessary to remain relevant in this market. After performing a “reverse” discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis, I ascertained that MongoDB needed to achieve, on average, revenue growth and FCF margins of 20% and 15%, respectively, to justify its valuation.

The following article revisits the stock to see if its slump is merely market noise or a signal that MongoDB’s business is in trouble.

Historical Trends

When I evaluate high-growth, technology companies, I am interested in how efficiently and sustainably they are growing. Looking back at MongoDB’s financial performance over the past five (fiscal) years, it scores a “Moderate” on my Growth Quality Dashboard, which evaluates seven critical metrics.

There are some concerning trends. For starters, MDB’s revenue growth is rapidly decelerating. Notably, this figure was just 19.22% for its fiscal year ending January 2025 (as represented by “2024” in the Growth Efficiency line chart above). This fell just short of my reverse DCF estimate of 20% and likely explains why the stock has underperformed since my last evaluation. Granted, a 35.8% CAGR is still solid, and the deceleration could simply be a maturing market. However, should the trend continue, I think it’s clear evidence that MDB is losing ground to its competitors. Moreover, although capital intensity has improved, MDB is still bogged down by heavy asset requirements relative to its peers in SaaS. R&D intensity is declining, but the absolute value has increased, and ~30% is a significant investment.

Valuation Assumptions

A few assumptions have changed since my last DCF analysis on MDB. For starters, its beta is much higher, coming in at 1.49 (5Y Monthly). Second, my terminal growth percentage (“perpetual growth rate”) estimate of 4 may have been too kind. 3% is more standard. “Year 1” revenue of $2.27 billion represents the consensus analyst estimate for MDB’s fiscal 2026. Revenue growth and FCF margin of 20% and 15%, respectively, represent averages during the nine-year projection. MDB’s recent FCF margin has been in the mid-to-high single digits, so 15% is a bit optimistic, but reasonably achievable for a SaaS company. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, despite its stock being down 48% in the past year, MDB is still quite expensive on a cash flow basis. Other metrics support this notion as well.

Given the deceleration of growth, MDB’s premium valuation is puzzling.

During my last look, MongoDB Atlas was seeing robust growth. However, in its fourth quarter fiscal 2025 report, Atlas's growth dropped to 24% year-over-year, making up 71% of total revenue. Total revenue was $548.4 million (up 20% year-over-year). Gross profit margin was 73%, compared to 75% a year prior. The company did generate $50.5 million in cash from operations, but this was no improvement over the same period in the prior year.

For its full year fiscal 2026, MongoDB is guiding for $2.24 billion and $2.28 billion. This would represent ~13% year-over-year growth. Notably, its non-Atlas business “will be down in the high-single digits for the year.” As for its first quarter guidance, MongoDB expects “Atlas revenue to be flat to slightly up sequentially.” This was impacted by “typical seasonality” and “holidays.”

Back to my DCF model, the following sensitivity analysis reveals the growth and margin expectations implied by MDB's valuation.

The path to outperformance is narrow. The market is pricing MDB for 27.2% growth. Even if margins improve to 22%, growth expectations remain over 20%.

Finally, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation to account for slight changes to key variables such as growth, margin, and beta. This gives us a more realistic and dynamic view of share price outcomes.

With the current assumptions, MDB is not merely overvalued, but grossly overvalued. This is evidenced by its current price of $191.29, trading over the 95th percentile of $183.96.

As for growth levers, MongoDB acquired Voyage AI “to enable organizations to build trustworthy AI applications” in February for $200 million. This will essentially serve to improve MongoDB’s existing platform and is not expected to contribute much to revenue in its fiscal year 2026.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, MongoDB’s growth is decelerating much faster than I anticipated, and this extends to Atlas, which is projected for ~20% growth. During its earnings call, MongoDB reiterated that its fiscal year 2026 will be a “transition year.” I may be a pessimist, but I think this is a subtle signal of things to come. To put it simply, things are not trending in the right direction. In parallel, market expectations remain sky-high (as evidenced by MDB's premium valuation) and out of touch. It’s hard to justify buying MDB in light of its issues (rating downgrade to “Hold” and “market underperform”). The growth deceleration points to market share erosion, and MDB's valuation is too high. Still, I can see MDB utility in a barbell portfolio, but this more like a speculative AI play than a strong and sustainable business.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Strengthened By MAG Silver Acquisition
data.description:
Introduction

With the current uncertainties in the market, there has been a switch to assets viewed as safe havens. Among them is gold (GLD), traditionally seen as the ultimate haven assets in times of uncertainty. Since the beginning of the year, the price of gold has risen 21.5%, resulting in a rise of 35% for the past year.

With a rising gold price comes a boost for gold miners. One such miner that has benefited is Pan American Silver (NYSE:PAAS). Whilst it has silver in its name, Pan American derives the majority of its revenue from the production of gold. Since my previous coverage last September, the shares have risen 6.7%, significantly ahead of the S&P 500, but lagging the growth seen in precious metal prices.

Whilst Pan American Silver’s jurisdictional profile does not appear the best at first glance, with only one mine in a tier-one jurisdiction [Canada], its other mines are spread across 6 jurisdictions. This results in it being one of the most geographically diversified precious metal miners.

With a solid set of results released for the first quarter of 2025, precious metal prices remaining high, and a recent acquisition of MAG Silver (MAG), let’s explore whether Pan American Silver remains a buy at this price.

Q1 Earnings

Pan American Silver reported its Q1-25 earnings at the start of May, reporting quarterly production of 5.0 million ounces of silver and 182,200 ounces of gold. This translated to no change in silver production compared to last year, but an 18% fall in gold production.

Gold production saw a dip, largely due to its disposition of the La Arena Mine in Peru in December 2024, contributing an 18,700-ounce reduction. In addition, lower grades at the Minera Florida Mine (2.29 g/t of gold vs. 2.97 g/t of gold) and Shahuindo Mine (0.44 g/t of gold vs. 0.50 g/t of gold), plus the Dolores mine entering a residual leaching phase following cessation of mining last July led to lower production. The other mines in the gold segment also reported smaller falls in grade and throughput.

In the silver segment, production was flat. Production fell at both the Cerro Moro mine and San Vicente mine due to mine sequencing leading to lower grade areas being mined. However, this was offset by the Huaron mine which, despite lower grades, reported increased production as throughput increased. Also increasing production was the La Colorada mine which saw 0.28 million ounces higher production driven by higher throughput following the completion of new ventilation infrastructure.

Despite production of silver being flat and gold production falling, revenues actually came in 29% higher than last year at $773.2 million. This was down to Pan American benefitting from a record realized gold price of $2,868 per ounce (+36% YoY) and higher realized silver prices of $31.25 per ounce (+38% YoY).

Based on this quarter's production, Pan American reaffirmed it remains on track to meet its 2025 outlook for gold and silver production of 735 to 800 thousand ounces and 20 to 21 million ounces respectively.

Pan American Silver reported a cash flow from operations of $240.1 million in the first quarter, an almost doubling from $127.1 million in the same quarter a year earlier. This translated into a free cash flow of $112.6 million. On the balance sheet side, Pan American had $923 million in cash and cash equivalents at quarter end, and a total debt of $804.4 million. This meant Pan American has a net-cash position indicating a very strong balance sheet.

Continuing to costs and margins, the company reported a first quarter all in sustaining cost [AISC] of $13.94 per ounce for the silver segment (-12% YoY) and $1,485 per ounce for the gold segment (-6% YoY).

The decrease in silver segment AISC was largely driven by the improved ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada leading to lower mining costs per tonne of ore and higher silver production. Cerro Moro mine saw lower AISC due the higher gold price increasing byproduct credits, with Huaron AISC also benefitting from increased byproduct credits from higher zinc and lead production. This fall in silver AISC was partially offset by a $2.08 increase in AISC per ounce at the San Vicente mine due to higher royalties and lower zinc byproduct credits.

In the gold segment, AISC also fell driven by the Dolores mine ceasing mining activities with Q1 2025 costs largely reflecting residual leaching activities which led to a much lower AISC. AISC rose significantly at Minera Flores (+35%) and Shahuindo (+18%) largely due to the lower grades produced. With the higher metal prices in the quarter, and lower AISC, this led to an increase in AISC margins.

Overall, this translated into net earnings of $169.3 million, compared to a net loss of $30.8 million a year earlier. Adjusted earnings also rose coming in at $153.0 million, up from $4.7 million last year and equivalent to earnings per share of $0.47. Pan American had a strong start to the year with the rising precious metal prices boosting earnings. We are seeing the new ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada pay off helping set Pan American up for what I believe will be a strong year.

MAG Silver Purchase

Like any commodity producer there is a finite life to Pan American Silver’s mines, with the eventual depletion of reserves inevitable. The Dolores mine has already shifted to a residual leaching following the end of mining and the Minera Florida mine has only a few years left of production based on current reserves.

Whilst Pan American has the large Escobal mine with over 250 million ounces of reserves and the potential for 20 million ounces a year of production, production has been suspended following a court ruling in Guatemala. The company continues its ILO 169 consultation process and meetings with the Guatemalan government, but progress has been slow, with no date for the restart of production.

With no plan for Escobal production restarting soon, Pan American faces a potential drop in production by the end of this decade. Whilst higher gold and silver prices may allow life extensions at some mines, new mines were required. This led Pan American Silver to recently announce the acquisition of MAG Silver in a $2.1 billion transaction.

For those less familiar, MAG Silver holds a 44% interest in the Juanicipio Mine, a high grade silver mine located in Zacatecus, Mexico. The remaining 56% is owned by the mine operator Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF). Juanicipio is one of the most profitable silver mines globally, with an AISC for 2025 forecast to be between $6 to $8. With proven & probable reserves of over 15 million tonnes at 628 g/t of silver equivalent, this is a high grade, low cost mine, with a initial 13 year mine life before we even consider the huge potential exploration upside.

Production is estimated to be 14.7 to 16.7 million ounces of Silver in 2025, with roughly 7 million ounces attributable to MAG Silver, on par with Pan Americans La Colorada mine, but at significantly better margins. At current spot prices, Juanicipio is expected to generate $98 million in cash flow for the 44% stake held.

Of course, the most important thing is the price paid. Pan American is paying $2.1 billion made up of $500 million in cash and 0.755 Pan American shares per MAG Silver Shares. With 15.4 million tonnes of Proven and Probable Mineral Reserves at grades of 248 g/t silver, 1.58 g/t gold, 2.64% lead and 4.80% zinc [628 g/t silver equivalent (AgEq)], this gives roughly 341.1 million ounces of silver in reserves for the Juanicipio mine. With a 44% stake in the mine, this attributes 150.1 million ounces of reserves to MAG Silver meaning Pan American paid $14 per ounce of silver equivalent reserves. Whilst this is higher than the roughly $10 per ounce Pan American Silver paid in 2018 for Tahoe Resources, it is significantly lower than the $25.70 per ounce reserves that peer Coeur Mining (CDE) paid for SilverCrest last year for a mine in the same jurisdiction [Mexico] and at slightly higher AISC.

In addition to the 44% stake in Juanicipio mine, the purchase of MAG Silver also adds 2 additional 100% owned exploration assets: Deer Trail in Utah and Larder in Ontario providing additional optionality in the future should exploration be successful. It is important to note that even the 7,679 hectare Juanicipio property is only 10% explored, with high-grade intercepts reported in step-out and regional drilling, giving potential long-term exploration upside.

Overall, this appears to be a good purchase for Pan American Silver. Whilst it cost $500 million in cash and 0.755 Pan American shares [roughly 78.5 million new shares issued increasing the share count by 21%] it comes at the same time as Pan American has been performing buybacks, authorizing buybacks of up to 5% of issued shares in 2025.

Given the price paid, the mine economics, and free cash flow Juanicipio will produce, this looks like an accreditive acquisition despite the share dilution. Juanicipio is a high-quality asset with industry leading margins, and a long life potential, something Pan American mines currently lack. With significant further upside possible from further exploration, I believe this is a good acquisition for Pan American Silver and helps position the company for a strong future.

Valuation

With a metal miner’s earnings being heavily reliant on the price of the underlying commodity they mine, an exact value for the miner will vary depending on the metal's price. In times of higher precious metal prices, all miners stand to benefit. However, each miner has a different starting value and operations that will impact future returns. As such, I believe one way to get the relative value of Pan American Silver is in comparison to its precious metal mining peers such as Coeur Mining (CDE), Endeavor Silver (EXK), Silvercorp Metals (SVM) AngloGold Ashanti (AU), Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Barrick Mining (B), Hecla Mining (HL), Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF) and First Majestic Silver (AG). Whilst all of these have significant precious metal operations, they all have large differences in cash and debt levels, so a direct comparison of earnings multiples will not suffice, and I instead use an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple.

Looking at the above we can see that Pan American Silver trades at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 5.70. This places it roughly middle of the pack compared to peers. However, we must also remember that Pan American is amongst one of the highest quality miners in this group with significant diversification with mines in over 6 jurisdictions, significant scale compared to smaller peers, and a history of strong management. When we consider that this metric is before interest and Pan American has a net cash position whereas peers have net debt positions leading to higher interest costs, Pan American appears even better. Despite trading in the middle of the pack, I am willing to argue that this is perfectly fair, and if anything, a slight premium could be justified.

To determine a price value for Pan American Silver, I calculate the estimated free cash flow and apply a multiple to this. I use the midpoint of guided production for 767,500 ounces of gold and 20.5 million ounces of silver given the company seems on track and the midpoint guided AISC. I assume a tax rate of 30%.

Assuming gold at $3,000 an ounce and silver at $32 an ounce, just below the current precious metal price, we get a free cash flow of $927 million in 2025. I apply a free cash flow multiplier of 10.7, the average of the mining sector [9.88] and Pan American’s 5-year average multiple [11.51], which gives a market cap of $9.9 billion. With 362.2 million shares currently outstanding, this gives a target price of $27.33. With the shares currently trading at $22.74 per share, Pan American appears to be undervalued.

If we assume the takeover of MAG Silver will result in the share count increasing to 440.5 million shares, this will translate to a target price of $22.47 per share, near the current share price. However, this fails to take into account the additional free cash flow produced by the Juanicipio mine which at an estimated $100 million in 2025 alone adds an additional $2.42 to the target share price making it $24.89. This is before we even consider the huge potential future upside the acquisition of MAG Silver presents from further mine expansion, exploration, and higher free cash flow in future years. Whilst it may impact the cash flow multiple derived target in the short term, in the long term it is likely to be highly accreditive.

It is important to remember that these calculations are done at precious metal prices lower than current prices, suggesting these calculations are more likely to be an underestimate. Additionally, I do not take into account the currently ongoing share buybacks which again boost the target share price. With the shares trading in-line with peers when a slight premium could be justified, and my calculations suggesting Pan American is undervalued at this price, I continue to believe that Pan American is a buy.

As with any investment, investing in Pan American Silver is not without its risk. With the company’s revenue and earnings dependence on the price of its underlying metals, I believe this is the primary risk. Any significant reduction in the price of gold, silver, or other byproducts will significantly reduce earnings and drive the share price lower. A further important risk is the mixed jurisdictional profile Pan American operates in. Whilst Canada may present little risk as a tier one, their other jurisdictions expose Pan American to sudden regulatory changes and political uncertainty, which could negatively impact operations and hence profitability. Just look at what happened with Pan American’s Escobal mine in Guatemala. One mitigation of this risk is the highly diversified nature of Pan American’s operations with no country representing more than 23% of revenue. This places it in a much stronger position than single-asset producers who risk revenue falling to zero overnight.

Conclusion

Pan American’s acquisition of MAG Silver marks a key addition to its portfolio. Pan American obtains a slice of a high-grade silver mine with some of the lowest costs in the world. This adds additional high-margin production in a mine with a long lifetime ahead. While the upfront cost is higher than previous acquisitions by Pan American and dilutes existing shareholders, in the long run, this looks highly accreditive with significant exploration upside potential.

With the latest results showing a strong performance, aided by the completion of new ventilation infrastructure at La Colorada, Pan American is in a strong position. Moreover, Pan American's continued share buybacks suggest management sees value at current levels and is actively working to offset any dilution.

With precious metal prices remaining near record levels, and one of the most diversified miners in the world, Pan American is well placed for the future. With my calculations continuing to suggest Pan American Silver remains undervalued at its current price, I continue to assign a buy rating.

data.title: Acadia Pharmaceuticals: Another Legal Win, ACP-101 Readout Preview
data.description:
Shares of Acadia Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ACAD) rallied 26% after the company secured another court win that further solidified Nuplazid’s intellectual property position.

The stock is now back to the low 20s and new 52-week highs, and while I did remove Acadia from our Growth Stock Forum model portfolio in late 2024 as part of downsizing and reprioritization efforts, it remains part of my Coverage Universe and I continue to see the company as well positioned for long-term value creation based on the steadily growing revenue base and cash flows and an expanding pipeline with two important readouts in the next 12 months.

Enrollment in ACP-101’s phase 3 trial in Prader Willi syndrome patients is ahead of schedule and Acadia now expects to report topline results in Q4 2025 versus the previous guidance for the first half of 2026, and ACP-204 (the next-generation Nuplazid) is progressing towards the first phase 2/3 trial readout next year, and it is expanding to Lewy body dementia psychosis with a phase 2 trial expected to start in the third quarter.

Another legal win solidifies Nuplazid’s long IP runway

Acadia’s share price rose 26% on Friday after the company secured another legal win on Nuplazid. This is the so-called “Pimavanserin II” case.

The company announced that the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware ruled in favor of Acadia on Nuplazid's ‘721 formulation patent. The court ruled in favor of Acadia on infringement and validity arguments in this case against Aurobindo Pharma and other ANDA filers. The ‘721 patent provides protection for the Nuplazid 34mg capsule formulation into 2038.

Acadia has previously settled with other generic filers that allowed the launch of the 34mg capsule in 2038 and of the 10mg tablet in September 2036, and this week’s court win ensures that this will remain the case (for the 34mg capsule) for generic filers that did not settle with the company.

What now remains on the legal side for Nuplazid is the appeal of MSN Pharmaceutical in the “Pimavanserin I” case related to patent ‘740 that Acadia won in late 2023. The appeal is fully briefed and the oral argument is scheduled for June 6, 2025.

Underwhelming but steady growth of commercial business

The bad news is that Daybue’s growth trajectory has not picked up in the last few quarters.

Acadia's increased efforts to improve Daybue’s traction and persistence are producing modest results. Net sales grew 11% Y/Y in the first quarter, and they declined sequentially due to the typical seasonal headwinds most pharmaceutical products face in the first quarter.

The good news is that Nuplazid is outperforming expectations and that total revenue growth expectations have finally found their footing. There were no downward revenue estimate revisions from analysts for the next four years in the last six months and expectations have slightly risen. The consensus for total revenues in the 2025-2028 period has improved by 1% to 4% compared to six months ago and by up to 1% in the last one and three months.

Rising expectations is one of the key ingredients for strong share price performance and while it is still largely lacking for Acadia, it is good to see that the downtrend was arrested and that it has slightly reversed.

I expect to see continued steady revenue growth going forward with potential acceleration in 2027 from potential new launches.

ACP-101’s phase 3 trial results are now expected in Q4 2025

The enrollment in ACP-101’s phase 3 trial is ahead of schedule and Acadia now expects to report topline results in the fourth quarter of this year versus the previous guidance for the first half of 2024.

I wrote about this trial in my February 2024 article to Growth Stock Forum subscribers and I will share a slightly abbreviated version today.

ACP-101 (formerly LV-101) is an intranasal version of carbetocin, an oxytocin analog designed to have improved oxytocin receptor selectivity and longer half-life compared with endogenous oxytocin. The candidate was gained from Levo Therapeutics in June 2022.

PWS is “characterized by profound hyperphagia, or an intense persistent sensation of hunger accompanied by food preoccupations and an extreme drive to consume food. Overt hyperphagia typically presents by 7 to 8 years of age and is associated with the lack of normal satiety response and a low basal metabolic rate.” Other psychiatric symptoms include increased anxiousness, obsessive-compulsive tendencies, and maladaptive behaviors. Oxytocin deficiency in these patients was believed to be associated with this constant sense of hunger in PWS patients.

ACP-101 is administered three times per day with meals. It was generally safe and well tolerated in phase 2 and phase 3 trials. Still, the phase 3 trial failed because the primary endpoints were based on the 9.6mg dose that did not statistically separate from placebo.

A positive efficacy signal was generated with the 3.2mg dose, and even though the effect of this dose was considered a secondary endpoint, Levo Therapeutics decided to submit the NDA. The Advisory Committee vote was negative (12 to 1 against approval) and the FDA ended up rejecting the application and asking for a new phase 3 trial in January 2022. Levo Therapeutics had no funds to do such a study and ultimately gave the rights to Acadia in June 2022 for $10 million upfront.

The efficacy signal of the 3.2mg dose looked good in the phase 3 trial, but the inverse dose-response is not exactly encouraging, nor is the failure of the 9.6mg dose to separate from placebo. Levo argued that the drug candidate has a U-shaped dose-response, and the FDA acknowledged the possibility but said these types of responses are rare.

The 9.6mg dose showed a trend on the HQ-CT endpoint in the phase 3 trial but it was far from reaching statistical significance and the effect of the 3.2mg seemed robust with a p-value of 0.016. The 3.2mg dose also achieved statistical significance on several secondary endpoints and it showed improvements in others compared to placebo. The table below shows how the 9.6mg dose also showed improvements on all endpoints, but without reaching statistical significance on any of the endpoints.

Responder analyses also favored the 3.2mg dose over the 9.6mg dose and placebo and the additional evidence of the 3.2mg dose working well was the placebo crossover period where patients crossing to the 3.2mg dose experienced significant improvements on the primary endpoint while the patients receiving the 9.6mg dose had no change.

There was also continued separation of the 3.2mg and 9.6mg doses in favor of the 3.2mg dose in the open-label extension part of the phase 3 trial.

The new phase 3 trial is slightly different and this adds to the risk of this phase 3 trial.

The length of treatment was extended from 8 weeks to 12 weeks and only the 3.2mg dose will be tested against placebo instead of two separate treatment groups. There is only one primary endpoint – the Hyperphagia Questionnaire for Clinical Trials, or HQ-CT, instead of two in the previous trial with CY-BOCS being relegated to a secondary endpoint.

Overall, I see a decent amount of evidence that supports further investigation of ACP-101 in PWS patients, but see the probability of success as below average for a phase 3 trial because of the issues from previous trials that I outlined here.

However, I do not see success of this trial as priced in at current levels and believe the risk-reward is good for patient long-term investors because I expect Acadia to do well in the long run even if ACP-101 fails and is discontinued.

In the meantime, we should see early evidence of commercial viability of PWS drugs from the launch of Soleno Therapeutics’ (SLNO) Vykat XR, which was approved by the FDA in March.

Risks

With most of the IP risks on Nuplazid in the rearview mirror, the key risks are the performance of the commercial business worsening and clinical trial failures, mainly of ACP-101 in PWS in Q4 2025 and of ACP-204 in Alzheimer's disease psychosis in mid-2026.

The standard biopharma macro risks also apply to Acadia, primarily potential pricing pressure for its drugs. However, I believe the risks to Acadia are lower than the average biopharma company because Nuplazid is not sold outside of the U.S. and is not subject to Most Favored Nation pricing risk that has resurfaced again, and with Acadia being well aware of such risks, it is unlikely to price Daybue far from the U.S. price in ex-U.S. territories.

Conclusion

Acadia's fundamentals are in better shape after another court win that all but secures Nuplazid's exclusivity until 2036 and after the stabilization of the commercial business following Daybue's worsening trajectory during late 2023 and the first half of 2025.

The key clinical catalyst is now expected in Q4 2025 with ACP-101 phase 3 data in PWS patients, and while I view it as not critical for the company's long-term success and the trial itself having below average probability of success based on the issues I outlined, I see the risk-reward as favorable for long-term investors that are willing to hold the stock beyond this event.

data.title: Fraport: Focus On Cash Flow Amidst High Debt Load
data.description:
Operating an airport is a lucrative business with high margins. So, they are quite appealing for investment. However, at the same time we do note that airports continue to invest in new projects and also spend significant amounts of money on maintenance. This provides a risk as new projects may face delays or higher input costs. Furthermore, airport operations are highly dependent on demand for air travel.

Fraport (OTCPK:FPRUF, OTCPK:FPRUY) is a company that is coming out of its CapEx heavy phase and has called off some smaller projects in an effort to bolster its cash position amidst rising labor costs. I covered the company in February and the stock has done rather well with a 15% return for the OTC ticker. The listing in Frankfurt (FWB:FRA) lost around 1%. So, there is a discrepancy between the OTC stock price development and the development of the underlying instrument. Around 5 points out of the 15% return is driven by exchange rate changes with the remainder being driven by lower volumes which at times causes the stock price not being reflective of the underlying instrument. That is also a reason why I believe that for serious investment considerations one should elect to invest in the most liquid instrument, which in the case of Fraport is the listing in Germany.

In this report, I discuss the most recent earnings, assess the guidance and the risks and opportunities that the company may face.

Fraport Shows Strong Performance Despite Slow Recovery At Frankfurt

Reported revenues were up 6% excluding construction revenues to €811 million. EBITDA declined 17% to €178 million. Excluding one offs, EBITDA would have been stable year-on-year. It is not the kind of performance we are looking for, but we do note that Frankfurt posted a decline in passenger traffic driven by weaker demand for destinations in the Middle East and West Europe. Generally, all European airports except for the ones in Greece posted declines in passenger traffic. While the Brazilian airports saw traffic increase except for Porto Alegre where traffic is still ramping up after the temporary flight suspension following the floods in 2024.

The increase in revenues was in part driven by high airport charges, fees and ground services. Frankfurt accounted for nearly 60% of the increase in adjusted revenues through higher charges and unit revenues. The remainder came most notably from Lima accounting for 20% of the increase while Fraport Greece and Fraport USA accounted for around 15% of the increase in revenues.

Operating expenses adjusted for construction costs increased 8.6% to €657.1 million due to higher concession charges driven by traffic volumes and higher labor costs. This was partially driven by higher headcounts, but we also note that the company reached a wage agreement effective from the first of April raising wages by 3% with a minimum increase of €110 per month and next year there will be another step up of 2.8% and an increase in the annual bonus. So, Fraport is facing labor costs pressures. I do expect the company to be able to pass through some of these pressures but that does not take away from the fact that in a more uncertain environment costs are rising.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Fraport?

For the full year, Frankfurt expects 64 million passengers which is an increase of nearly 4%. Compared to the pre-pandemic traffic volumes, the company is only 90% recovered. The slow recovery is driven by numerous factors. One of the factors is the soft demand from flights to China and in Q1 we saw soft demand for destinations in the Middle East and West Europe. Furthermore, operators including Lufthansa is facing delivery delays on narrow body and wide body airplanes which is constraining growth at the Frankfurt hub. Wizz Air is facing delivery delays as well which primarily affects the airports in Bulgaria (Burgas and Varna) while these Sunny Beach destinations also do not seem to be preferred leisure destinations.

We also have to monitor the geopolitical and trade setting, which next to adverse weather, can affect operations and demand. Costs at Frankfurt continue to rise due to labor costs and requirements to blend in Sustainable Aviation Fuel, which does increase the costs of operations for airlines in Frankfurt.

EBITDA is still expected to grow modestly this year supported by the opening of the new terminal at Lima in May and the new terminal opened in Antalya. Terminal 3 in Frankfurt will be opened next year allowing for a revamp of Terminal 2, which will have its operations moved to Terminal 3. Given that Frankfurt is not yet operating at full capacity, this temporary closure of Terminal 2 should pose no issues from capacity perspective.

As growth is somewhat challenged this year on group level, the company has elected to adapt its CapEx plan accordingly. The company is focusing on quick win projects with lower capital need while it extended the planning phase for the Terminal 2 revamp, effectively reducing near-term CapEx.

So, the company has some growth ahead through the recovery of Frankfurt and the new terminal at Lima but we also see some challenges due to labor increases and macroeconomic pressures.

Fraport Stock Has Upside Amidst Challenges

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The median EV/EBITDA for Fraport would suggest a negative stock price valuation due to the high debt load that the company is facing. However, the debt has been compounded through the CapEx heavy phase which is now largely behind us. Particularly from FY26 onward we should be seeing the company being able to significantly reduce its gross debt and leverage should already come down this year.

This year, the company will be refinancing the majority of its Frankfurt related debt maturities this year. In combination with the rough operating environment where Frankfurt is not quite living up to recovery expectations while costs are rising, I am assigning a hold rating with a retrospective price target of $71.05, which is a significant reduction from the prior target of $85.15. This is driven by lower peer group median that I am applying to transportation infrastructure names.

The book value per share is $57.10. Typically the company trades at 1.4x its book value and the peer group trades at 1.26x book value. That would imply a stock price target of $71.94 to $80. With that in mind, I am maintaining my buy rating but I am lowering my price target to $71.05.

Conclusion: Fraport Will Face A Challenging Year

I believe that Fraport will face a challenging year as its Frankfurt operations are not quite recovering amidst geopolitical challenges as well as shortages of airplanes and higher costs. However, from valuation point-of-view I do believe that the typical price-to-book valuation provides upside and enough reason to maintain my buy rating. I also believe that part of this year’s debt maturities will be refinanced for the sole purpose to build in some liquidity to pay a dividend after 2025.

In some way, for Fraport despite the challenges it is harvesting season. CapEx is moderating and the focus is now on improving the free cash flow.

data.title: POET Technologies: Potential Remains, But So Do The Risks (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

POET Technologies (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:POET), a Canadian firm developing photonic integrated circuits and optical engines, is working to reshape how optical data moves across systems. Its business centers on the Optical Interposer platform, which is designed to combine photonics and electronics on a single chip. The goal is faster, more energy-efficient connectivity, mainly for AI, cloud infrastructure, and large-scale data centers.

Back in September, I rated POET Technologies a Buy. The call was based on a few key factors, mainly its partnership with Mitsubishi Electric and what looked like solid momentum in the 800G and 1.6T optical engine markets. At first, the market agreed. POET increased +50% by the end of the year. But that run didn’t last. The stock has since pulled back and now trades about 4% below where I first got in. So the original thesis hasn’t been invalidated, but it’s definitely facing a tougher test now.

This week’s Q1 2025 earnings report places that original investment thesis in sharper relief. With early product shipments now in motion, a purpose-built high-volume facility operational in Malaysia, and a strong industry reception at OFC 2025, POET appears to be transitioning from blueprint to execution. My view is that POET is now entering a defining stretch in which its long-touted promise must begin to materialize as repeatable revenue and operational leverage.

POET’s Revenue Growth

POET Technologies’ Q1 2025 revenues increased from a barely-there $8,710 a year ago to $166,760 this quarter, a 1,813.74% surge. It marks the first signs of actual monetization from non-recurring engineering services and early product shipments, particularly in the 400G and 800G transceiver segments, exactly the areas where AI infrastructure and hyperscale data centers are pouring capital.

The company also posted $0.08 in earnings per share, beating consensus by $0.14 and reversing a loss of $0.13 from the same quarter last year, not to mention the gaping $0.50 per-share loss from just one quarter ago. However, this sudden profitability was largely driven by a $15.4 million non-cash gain from the revaluation of derivative warrant liabilities. This is a sharp contrast to the $12.4 million loss on the same line item in the previous quarter and a $630,000 loss in the same period last year. This type of gain doesn’t mean the company itself is suddenly more profitable in the usual sense. But even so, it makes the earnings look much better on paper, which can change how investors see the company and reinforces POET's ability to leverage financial structuring as it scales.

These earnings were further buoyed by a significant increase in interest and other income, rising tenfold from $52,000 in Q1 2024 to $528,000 this quarter, underscoring improved treasury management and a stronger cash position heading into a critical commercial ramp-up phase.

POET’s Product Shipments Begin

The numbers, while modest, have laid the groundwork. But what matters more is that POET finally shipped final design samples of its Infinity line—400G FR4, 800G 2xFR4, and 800G DR8—to three major tech customers. These are late-stage validation shipments, and they came out of the company’s high-volume facility in Malaysia, which signal that the company is moving from prototype to production in markets that demand reliability at scale.

At the 2025 Optical Fiber Communications Conference, POET debuted of its POET Teralight engines and the Blazar external light. Blazar, in particular, is aimed at a long-standing cost bottleneck in AI architecture: chip-to-chip optical links. Hyperscale data centers are already drowning in latency and overhead as they try to scale up bandwidth for AI workloads. If POET’s tech really can shift that equation, it matters a lot.

But maybe even more important than the technology is the structure behind it. POET is trying to control the ecosystem they come from. It finalized a 10,000-square-foot wafer-level cleanroom in Malaysia and fully acquired Super Photonics Xiamen [SPX]. Together, those form the backbone of a “China Plus One” strategy, which is about minimizing geopolitical exposure while tightening the feedback loop between design, testing, and production. So instead of sending pieces of the process to third parties and hoping it all syncs, POET is building an integrated line it controls, an unusual level of verticality in a microcap hardware name.

Which brings me to the $25 million equity raise. The round was done at a 21.8% premium to market, had no middlemen extracting fees, and was filled by a single institutional investor. I think this configuration could be viewed as a clear signal of conviction. The fact that this capital is earmarked for general growth and working capital, not debt repayment or survival runway, positions the raise as proactive and opportunistic, not reactive.

POET’s Valuation

If you were throwing money at speculative plays like POET and anything with an AI label last fall, you probably remember the mood was a lot looser. Risk was in, everyone was seeing upside. But then a Trump 2.0 administration kicked off a fresh round of confusion, complete with market shocks tied to a reignited tariff war. Appetite turned to indigestion pretty fast. So right now, in this new context, there’s a particular kind of tension that defines POET Technologies: a company with almost no visible earnings track record and barely any revenue to speak of, somehow commanding a $350 million market cap and a share price hovering at $4.48.

On paper, it shouldn’t work. The first P/E estimate doesn’t show up until 2027, at a speculative 19.48x. That’s because, as these speculative, high-risk plays go, POET is trading on story. But in fairness, the story has legs.

Analysts tracking the company are projecting an annual rate of return of 31.99%, thanks to a forecasted revenue explosion of 302% year-over-year.

For context, the sector median sits at just 6.78%, which puts POET’s supposed trajectory over 4,300% above industry norms.

But revenue actually declined 32.07% year-over-year prior to this. So the promise of triple-digit growth has to leap over the fact that, until very recently, the business was shrinking.

This gap between potential and performance came into full view in the company’s Q1 2025 earnings. On the surface, things looked great: a net profit of $6.3 million and a headline shift into the black. But that profit came almost entirely from that non-cash fair value gain on warrant liabilities, a technical accounting swing, not real economic output. Strip that away, and the company is still losing money.

More telling is the revenue figure. For the quarter, POET posted just $166,670 in sales, falling short of analyst expectations by $232,380. So even as the company gains visibility through tech demos, new product lines, and expanded manufacturing capacity, it hasn’t shown the ability to convert any of that into consistent, scalable revenue.

Weak Revenue Base

There’s a kind of suspended reality surrounding POET Technologies right now, the kind where the machinery of future success is being loudly assembled even as the present business model fails to support it. This is a company staging for scale, ramping CapEx, building out working capital, pushing out new tech, but still struggling to show that anyone’s actually buying what it’s selling.

Start with the top line. Let's go back to the fact that a large chunk of what POET books comes from non-recurring engineering contracts. These are one-off custom deals, not durable product sales. So while they help create the illusion of commercial activity, they don’t build a customer base or a revenue stream that can compound over time. That distinction matters, because without repeat business or widespread adoption, you’re just freelancing.

Going over the financials, company’s cash flow profile deepens my concern. Operating cash burn in Q1 2025 reached $8.9 million, worsening from $4.6 million the year before and even slightly exceeding the $8.7 million burned in Q4 2024.

On a trailing twelve-month basis, POET has torched $27.7 million, up from $23.3 million a year prior. It’s accelerating in the wrong direction.

To keep the engine running, POET has defaulted to one option: equity issuance. In the TTM, more than $80 million has been raised by issuing stock, which has kept operations afloat but steadily diluted existing shareholders. In effect, the company is paying its bills by cutting the same pie into smaller and smaller slices. That model works for a while, but only if there’s a clear path to organic revenue, and that path still isn’t visible.

Yet, the balance sheet still has some breath in it. POET holds $48.64 million in cash and carries just $7.32 million in debt, giving it a net cash position and some protection from credit market swings.

This cushion provides room to maneuver, but it’s also misleading. Because unless revenue catches up [see above], the company will inevitably have to go back to the market to raise more. And every time it does, the ownership math gets worse for current investors.

Meanwhile, capital expenditures are sending a very different signal. Over the last twelve months, POET spent $7.3 million on CapEx, a notable increase over past norms.

Working capital has exploded 796% year-over-year. In isolation, these figures would suggest a company gearing up for real scale, confident that large orders and recurring revenue are just around the corner. But when stacked against very weak revenues, non-recurring sales, and worsening cash burn, the investments feel more speculative than strategic.

Rising R&D Burden

The cost structure also reflects the turbulence of transition. In Q1 2025, R&D spending spiked to $4.3 million, more than double what the company spent a year ago and up sharply from $3.4 million just last quarter. On one level, that’s par for the course in high-tech ventures. Even so, the size and inconsistency of the increase point to a company having a hard time balancing its product development efforts with the push to get those products to market. And it’s not limited to R&D. The quarter also included $0.8 million in stock-based compensation and $0.7 million in depreciation, which added to expenses without doing anything to help cash flow.

Meanwhile, POET’s acquisition of the remaining 24.8% of Super Photonics Xiamen [SPX] is clearly central to POET’s commercial roadmap, so consolidating full control makes strategic sense. But the deal carried a $6.85 million non-cash loss, a hit that reflects the cost of ambition and, more crucially, the risks baked into POET’s capital allocation strategy. My concern is that it’s one thing to invest in vertical integration when revenues are scaling. It’s another when sales are still limping in from irregular engineering contracts.

Despite everything, the company still carries an “A” Growth Grade. But without any consensus analyst coverage on core profitability metrics, no EBITDA, EBIT, or EPS forecasts, there’s no critical wall pushing back on that narrative which makes it a lot easier to believe the projections when no one is checking the math. All of this funnels toward a projected share price of $9.29 which implies over 100% upside from where it trades today [see the FAST Graphs "Forecasting" data above].

Final Verdict

POET Technologies gets a Hold from me, down from a Buy. The tech is interesting, the long-term pitch still makes sense, and the strategy isn’t without merit. But there’s still no real product revenue to speak of, the cash burn hasn’t slowed, and most of the recent gains came from accounting maneuvers, not operations. Until the company starts delivering results that aren’t propped up by financial smoke, I’m on the sidelines.

data.title: Tech Is Crushing Dividends - And I Love It
data.description:
Over the past several years, technology stocks have been taking the market by storm. In sharp contrast, non-tech dividend stocks like those found in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), as well as higher-yielding sectors like REITs (VNQ) and utilities (XLU), have trailed significantly:

While earlier this year there appeared to be a major market reversal, with dividend stocks taking the lead over tech while the Nasdaq (QQQ) experienced a sharp pullback, over the past several weeks tech seems to have renewed its rapid growth and is now leaving dividend stocks in the dust once again.

As most of my regular readers know, I invest primarily in dividend stocks. As a result, you may think that I am discouraged by this turn of events—and possibly even reconsidering my investing strategy. However, in this article, I will detail why I could not be happier that tech stocks are crushing dividend stocks right now.

Why Tech Stocks Are Crushing Dividend Stocks

The biggest reason why tech stocks have crushed dividend stocks over the past several years is that artificial intelligence has emerged as a revolutionary technology that is opening up enormous growth potential for mega-cap technology stocks—whether it be NVIDIA (NVDA) as the leading supplier of AI-enabling semiconductors, or Microsoft (MSFT) with its aggressive investments in AI products and researchers, including into OpenAI, or even Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), Apple (AAPL), and Amazon (AMZN), which are also making very aggressive bets on AI technologies and AI-enabled products and applications. Even car companies like Tesla (TSLA) are emerging as AI behemoths due to their rapid advances in self-driving technology and aggressive investments in autonomous robots that should have enormous value to consumers and corporations. As such, it is evident that the tech space is booming due to AI's ascendancy and apparent readiness for prime time.

Meanwhile, dividend stocks have been weighed down by long-term interest rates being higher for longer, as well as uncertainties about trade policy. While this trade uncertainty certainly slammed tech stocks as well earlier this year, it also hurts long-dividend-paying stocks due to the fact that many of them are multinational in nature and also, in some cases, are very involved in the energy sector (XLE)—which was significantly hurt by the potential for tariffs and a corresponding economic downturn.

Today, however, the threat of a major economic disruption from tariffs has subsided substantially due to a thawing of tensions between the U.S. and China—which are the world's two largest economies—as well as a deal between the United States and the United Kingdom and the potential for many more major trade agreements in the coming days and weeks. That said, long-term interest rates remain relatively high, and the Fed is showing no signs of wanting to cut interest rates in the near future due to sustained economic performance in the United States and continued fears that tariffs may cause inflation to reaccelerate. This is weighing on dividend stocks in general because they tend to be much more interest rate-sensitive than tech stocks. The yield generated by dividend stocks typically competes with bond yields as an income source for income-focused investors, leading to a material headwind for dividend stocks whenever bond yields rise.

There are additionally many dividend-paying stocks—especially real asset ones like REITs, utilities, infrastructure, and midstream companies—that all use a good bit of leverage. Therefore, when interest rates are higher, their cost of capital is also higher. Meanwhile, most mega-tech stocks are cash-rich, have little debt on their balance sheets, and do not pay much in the way of dividends, so none of these headwinds affect them nearly to the same degree that they impact dividend stocks.

Why I Am So Happy About It

So with all that bad news for dividend stocks, why am I so happy that tech stocks are crushing dividend stocks—especially given that my dividend stock exposure significantly outweighs my tech stock exposure? Well, the answer is simply that I plan to continue being a net buyer of dividend stocks relative to tech stocks—and certainly relative to holding cash—over time. Therefore, I am very excited that I continue to have golden opportunities to buy high-quality, high-yield stocks with strong balance sheets and underlying fundamentals at very attractive valuations.

Sure, it feels good in the short term to see a lot of green in my portfolio and make a lot of money. To be clear, I have been able to outperform the S&P 500 by a significant margin over the course of my investing career strictly through investing in dividend stocks. However, my future returns depend a great deal on the price at which I am able to allocate fresh capital today. And since dividend stocks by and large remain very attractively valued compared to tech stocks, this is great news for dividend investors like me—because it means we are able to buy additional shares at much more compelling valuations than tech stock investors are today.

In fact, even if I were not an individual stock picker, I still would be much more inclined to invest in dividend stocks than in tech stocks. While mega-cap-dominated indexes like the S&P 500 trade at nosebleed valuation levels based on multiple valuation models—such as the Buffett Indicator, the S&P mean reversion model, and the S&P 500 price-to-earnings model—prominent dividend growth stock ETFs like SCHD trade at very attractive dividend yields relative to their recent history, while also being filled with high-quality blue-chip names that have powered it to an 11% dividend CAGR over the past decade.

Additionally, in the individual dividend stock universe, there are many attractive opportunities, including the alternative asset management space, where you can combine growth and yield with stocks like Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) and Blue Owl Capital (OWL), trading at very reasonable valuations and combining dividend yields that significantly beat what is offered by the broader market today—along with strong high-teens expected dividend per share growth for the foreseeable future.

You can also invest in the infrastructure space with many attractive opportunities across the midstream (AMLP) and other AI-related infrastructure segments, including the likes of Energy Transfer (ET) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC). You also can buy a lot of quality REITs at deep discounts to their intrinsic value, as evidenced by the private market value of their assets. An example of this is Realty Income (O), as per my recent analysis here. You can also buy quality infrastructure funds like the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) at a very reasonable price-to-earnings multiple and a 7% dividend yield, along with a stellar track record and prudent leverage.

Investor Takeaway

Tech stocks are hot right now—and have been for the past several years—and I certainly do not think without reason either, given that AI does have enormous potential to radically transform the economy. However, as a dividend investor, I could not be happier with the recent underperformance of the dividend stock space. This is because I plan to buy a lot more dividend stocks in the coming years, and I think the valuations being offered today are extremely compelling, especially relative to the broader marketplace.

Additionally, even in recent years, if I were simply a retiree, I would still be happy about the underperformance because I would be living off my dividends. I would not have to care one way or the other about the underlying stock price performance as long as my dividends continue to roll in and grow at a pace that beats inflation over time. This would especially be the case if my portfolio generated extra dividends above and beyond my living expenses—because then I could grow my passive income stream even faster than I would have been able to otherwise by reinvesting at higher yields.

As a result, while tech stock investors may be rejoicing today at their outsized returns, we are equally happy at the attractive opportunities Mr. Market is giving us in the high-yield space today at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Arbor Realty: Dividend Unlikely To Recover After 2025 As Multifamily Outlook Worsens
data.description:
In January, I published a bearish outlook on the multifamily and commercial property lender Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) in "Arbor Realty: Short Opportunity As Southern U.S. Housing Glut Forms." My view was that high interest rates combined with signs that the housing shortage is ending would eventually cause lower rents, pushing delinquency risk higher. Since then, ABR has declined by about 23% while the S&P 500 is roughly unchanged.

Arbor declined sharply in February after its Q4 earnings missed estimates, with a staggering 20% year-over-year decline in sales. Its Q1 earnings were slightly above estimates but failed to improve sentiment due to a notable decline in loan originations and net interest income. Since then, the company's manager has noted increased focus on reducing leverage costs to offset rising delinquencies; however, its core business model remains generally unchanged, and many investors and analysts remain concerned about continued dividend cuts.

Arbor's 2025 EPS estimate has declined to $0.81, with targets ranging from $0.57 to $1.32, giving the company a forward price-to-earnings ratio of about 13X. That also means its reduced dividend ($1.2 annualized) is still likely not covered. Based on the company's guidance and analyst estimates, most expect Arbor's prospects to begin recovering in 2026.

Its recovery depends primarily on what happens next in its primary operating market. Arbor largely depends on the high-risk bridge loan market with higher LTVs that may not be easily refinanced to agency-backed CMBS loans, given bearish commercial property market conditions. Given its high leverage, a continued increase in non-performing loans can theoretically compromise its remaining book value or pose challenges in selling or improving foreclosed properties.

Arbor cannot Reduce Leverage Fast Enough

Over the past five years, Arbor has tried to reduce its effective leverage. Around 2022, when rising rates began to pressure the firm, it had total assets of nearly 11X its tangible book value. Today, that figure has declined to 6.9X, which is still notable, offset by more than doubling its expected loan loss rate. See below:

Although this rate has not increased markedly over the past year, it failed to decline as many had hoped. That said, its overall balance of non-performing loans exceeding 60 days past due fell to $511M in Q1 from $651M in Q1 2024. That said, its total unpaid principal balance on loans with modifications was a staggering $949M at the end of Q1, representing ~8.3% of its total structured business loans. In most of these loans, Arbor required borrowers to invest additional capital to provide rate relief.

Arbor has also seen a shocking buildup in Real Estate-Owned "REO," which is now a net total of $302M, up from $176M in Q4 2024. This is from nine multifamily properties, two office buildings, and two land parcels after seven Q1 foreclosures. In general, foreclosures do not provide sufficient capital to offset loans because these properties are likely foreclosed on because their value is falling, in addition to selling costs, rehab, and property management challenges.

A slow decline in multifamily lending stability threatens Arbor's core business model. We're seeing high levels of loan modifications as it looks to keep its delinquency rate from rising. While that limits its direct risk, its ability to refinance these loans into agency-backed CMBS originations is becoming compromised. In 2023, I specifically detailed this issue regarding its "Agency Takeout" Model. The company makes money by refinancing high-risk bridge loans into lower-risk agency CMBS. However, that is not possible if LTVs on its bridge loans rise due to the combination of lower property values, higher interest rates, and modified loans.

Multifamily Property Outlook Worsens

Arbor Realty is doing what it can to manage this strain, mainly through aggressively lowering its leverage. In my view, its decision to reduce its dividend is also a wise preservation of capital. That said, most of its assets are concentrated in sunbelt multifamily properties, which are generally riskier, given that around 96% are bridge loans.

In its last investor call, managers saw 2025 as a "transition year" with a revised dividend and a buildup in REO inventories. That said, there is limited evidence to suggest that fundamental negative economic headwinds will fade by the end of the year. Yes, the market is not looking for a rise or decline in the SOFR rate (set by the Fed), which has been the primary factor weighing on the market, given commercial mortgage rates are around 7-8% today. Overall, US commercial property prices fell as rates rose, rebounded, and slipped again last month, potentially pointing to another leg lower.

Looking at economic fundamentals that impact Arbor, concentrated in the sunbelt state multifamily, the outlook appears worse. Since I covered Arbor last, we've seen a continued increase in the southern US rental vacancy rate and seasonal increases in home inventories:

The "housing shortage" has pushed rents higher, offsetting extremely low property capitalization rates and high borrowing costs with high NOI growth. I believe many investors and analysts expect the housing shortage to push rents up forever. However, in the South, asking rents begin to decline as the shortage transitions into a glut. Since I covered Arbor last, condo sales have dropped with slightly lower prices. Developers are pulling back construction activity, while the hiring rate continues to plummet, signaling a general economic contraction and alleviation of the housing shortage.

These data are only slightly worse than when I last covered Arbor, but they continue to point to long-term strain in the multifamily residential market. Although interest rates are unlikely to rise, the Federal Reserve is not expected to continue cutting rates beyond 25 bps.

Housing construction levels, though falling, are still relatively high compared to the 2010s norm. In my view, this data indicates likely overdevelopment of properties, particularly multifamily, that will not be met with sufficient demand. Rents are not rising while inflation is likely pushing property managers' overhead costs, harming NOIs.

It is likely true that, as CEO Ivan Kaufman mentioned, the initial occupancy issues related to COVID and post-COVID are improving. That said, I disagree with his take that the market is bottoming out overall. Although COVID rent-forbearance properties created strains, those challenges were heavily offset by skyrocketing rents and low rates around 2020-2022. The market faces likely sustained rates around 4%, while we're seeing emerging signals of lower economic demand and excessive housing development, particularly in the Southern US.

The Bottom Line

To me, the fact that Arbor is performing so poorly during a relatively decent economic period is alarming. Most economic data suggests that the challenges facing the multifamily development sector continue to mount. The best Arbor can do is likely seek to sell REO properties quickly and reduce its leverage so its book value will not suffer from what I expect to be a continued increase in non-performing loans.

My bearish thesis on Arbor remains intact, and I expect ABR to lose value. Since I last covered it, I argue that economic conditions facing the multifamily market have worsened. However, rents and home values are not clearly falling, suggesting that Arbor may have adequate time to continue improving its liquidity and solvency profile.

Though I remain bearish, I would not bet against it at its current price. If the Federal Reserve shifts its focus and cuts rates more aggressively as Trump aims for, Arbor's health could improve, which may alleviate strain for Arbor's borrowers. Still, I see limited evidence for a return to pre-2022 interest rates, and considerable evidence that the multifamily residential property market will be comparatively slow for the next ten years compared to the past ten.

data.title: Cronos Reports Improved Financials For Q1-2025 As Demand Outpaces Supply
data.description:
Cronos (NASDAQ:CRON) reported increasing revenues and net income for Q1-2025. Its international and domestic sales have increased. The company’s stock is up 25% over the last month. It is up 6.5% since I covered it last March on Seeking Alpha. While the company boasts top market share in Israel, there are some new developments which incur risk. I continue my rating of a Hold for the company.

Q1-2025 Results

Cronos reported revenues of $32.3 million, a 27.7% increase YoY and a 6.6% increase QoQ. GrowCo revenues totaled $2.9 million and Cronos totaled $29.4 million. Increase in revenues was due to higher sales in Israel, internationally, and in Canada. The company brought in $9.2 million in revenues from Israel for the quarter. Gross profit was $13.7 million or 43% margin. Gross profit has increased 204% YoY and 52% QoQ.

Operating expenses have decreased YoY and QoQ. Cash and short-term investments were $838 million, a decrease QoQ due to CapEx spending. The company is expanding the GrowCo facilities with the expenditures. It is expected the CapEx will remain elevated as the company cures supply constraints. Cronos remains debt free. The company reported a net income of $6.1 million compared to a net loss of $2.2 million YoY. The company did not report free cash flow for the quarter.

Market consensus for Q2-2025 revenue is $32.33 million, representing an increase YoY and no change QoQ. The company will report next earnings in August.

Current Synergies and Risk

Cronos is a large Canadian cannabis LP with an international footprint and a large portfolio of brands. Cronos maintains the top market share of the medical cannabis market in Israel. Cronos also has cannabis operations in the UK, Germany, and Australia. The company’s Peace Naturals brand covers its international operations.

The company’s Canadian Spinach brand holds second place in terms of market share in Canada. The brand has been so popular that the company has experienced supply constraints. The company is expanding its GrowCo operations to meet the demand. The company acquired half ownership of GrowCo last quarter and invested $51 million in expansion efforts for their grow facility. Cronos buys 80% of GrowCo’s harvests.

Cronos had record sales in Israel for the quarter. In April, the Israeli government decided to impose up to 165% tariffs on Canadian cannabis. The tariffs have not been fully approved yet. The tariffs come in response to the government accusing Cronos of “dumping” its products in their market. This charge assumes that the company is lowering its prices below the fair market value of its export country. Cronos denies any truth in the allegations, but tariffs may still be imposed. The tariffs will bring some risk to the company’s international sales.

The company has reported net income over the last three quarters. The company is not consistently reported free cash flow. The company’s assets ($1.1 trillion) far outweigh its liabilities ($39.1 million). The company’s cash and short-term investments are higher than any of the other Canadian cannabis LPs.

The company and its stock price are undervalued. Its market cap ($806.28 million) is less than its total books value ($992.5 million). The company’s stock price ($2.14) trades below its book value per share ($2.77) and NAV per share value ($2.89). The stock price trades about $1 below its 52-week high ($3.12). Weak investor sentiment in the cannabis sector will keep the price undervalued. Technically, US cannabis reform will not immediately affect Cronos, since it has zero US operations.

Although cannabis stocks have been down over the last twelve months, there has been a small rally recently. Over the last thirty days, Village Farms is up 83%, Canopy Growth up 66%, Organigram Global up 33%, Cronos up 25%, Tilray up 7%, and SNDL up 1.5%. In terms of 30-day performance, Village Farms, Canopy Growth, and Ogranigram Global are in the lead. The stock prices are gaining due to larger market conditions, like temporary reprieves from the trade wars, as well as from the possibility of new cannabis regulations in the US. Cronos leads the group in terms of total assets but lags the group in terms of revenues. Cronos is amongst the only three companies which have been reporting net income. Cronos’ balance sheet is the strongest compared to its peers. The sector overall remains weak and has negative investor sentiment. These thirty-day gains may easily be erased due to larger market volatility.

Investment Strategy and More Risk

Cronos is at low risk of poor financial performance. The tariffs in Israel will affect the company’s international profits as prices will go up. Overall, it would only negatively impact some of its revenues and profits. The company’s strong balance sheet and liquidity insure future success with its business strategy of international expansion. Cronos has managed to increase its revenues and the trend may continue.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a moderate to high risk. The stock price is down over the year and may continue going down. Besides a weak cannabis sector, greater stock market volatility due to the trade wars will continue. The price may decrease more before new uptrends emerge. The company’s strong financial performance will not change cannabis sector volatility.

Conclusion

Cronos reported higher revenues and net income for Q1. The company is expanding its grow operations to meet the increasing demand. Cronos expects demand and improved financial results to continue for the year. There is a new risk with tariffs in Israel and, if passed, the company may be negatively affected. Overall, the company has strong financial performance and will survive any flux in its international operations. I rate the company as a Hold due to sector weakness and greater market volatility.

data.title: BUI: 6.7% Yield On Defensive Utilities (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Amid all of the chaos, Utilities are having a good year so far in 2025 - they're up nearly 8%, as investors scramble for defensive positions to protect their portfolios from market volatility.

If you're interested in plugging into the Utilities sector, but don't have the time to research these companies, there are several closed-end funds, or "CEF's," that offer exposure to this sector, such as the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (NYSE:BUI).

Fund Profile:

BUI's

"investment objective is to provide total return through a combination of current income, current gains and long-term capital appreciation. The Trust seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing primarily in equity securities issued by companies that are engaged in the Utilities, Infrastructure, and Power Opportunities business segments anywhere in the world and by utilizing an option writing (selling) strategy in an effort to enhance current gains." (BUI site)

BUI began in 2011. It held 54 positions, as of 4/30/25, with average daily volume of 56K, and net assets of $514M. The expense ratio is 1.08%, with scant leverage involved.

Holdings:

As you'd expect, Utilities are BUI's largest sector exposure, at ~48%. Capital Goods follows, at 27.44%, with Energy at 10.48%. It has less than 5% exposure to several other sectors and sub-sectors, such as Commercial & Professional Services, Materials, and Transportation:

Its main regional exposure is the U.S., at 62%, distantly followed by France, at 9.3%, the UK, at 7.8%, Italy, at 5%, and Canada, at 3.7%. It also has exposures of less than 3% each in several other countries:

BUI's top 10 positions include Next Era Energy (NEE) the biggest market cap U.S. utility; and National Grid (NGG), comprising over 9% of BUI's portfolio.

BUI also has positions in GE Vernova (GEV), a relative newcomer, founded in 2021; SSE, formerly Southern and Scottish Energy; Trane Technologies (TT), an Irish company with worldwide operations, founded in 1871; in addition to midstream energy giant Williams Companies (WMB) and LNG firm Cheniere Energy. Multinational Enel Spa, French construction company Vinci, and Elia Group, which operates transmission systems in Belgium and Germany.

Large cap stocks dominate BUI's portfolio, at ~94%, with mid caps at 4.7%.

The U.S. dollar (DXY) is the main currency exposure, at 63.5%; followed by the Euro, at 21.9%. The U.S. $ is down ~7% vs. the Euro so far in 2025.

Dividends:

BUI pays monthly distributions - it generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays at the end of the month. At its intraday 5/16/25 price of $24.34, BUI yielded 6.7%.

Management raised the monthly payout by 12% for 2025, to $.1360 from $.1210, where it had been since 2014.

In 2024, BUI's NII declined ~17%, while its Net Realized Gains rose rose 29%, and Unrealized Appreciation fell ~57%. Net Assets decreased $32.5M in 2023 and $32.99M in 2024:

Taxes:

23.75% of 2024 distributions were characterized as Return of Capital, "ROC." ROC offers you a tax deferral benefit, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell you shares.

Performance:

2020 and 2021, COVID-impacted years, were strong performance periods for BUI, as investors fled to defensive sectors. However, rising interest rates pressured BUI's NAV and market price returns in 2022. In 2023 and 2024, BUI's performance returned to positive territory.

As of 3/31/25, BUI's 5-year returns were its strongest, at 11.63% for NAV and 12.93% for Market Price. Its 10-year returns were lower, at 7.43% for NAV, and 8.70% for price.

Its 3-year returns were its lowest, due to the decline in 2022, while its 1-year 7.72% NAV return is a bit above its 10-year mark, and its 1-year 12% price return is much higher than its 10-year 8.7% figure:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: Rising interest rates would pressure BUI's portfolio value, as they did in 2022, when BUI's market price fell by ~17%.

Economic Risk: A recession could reduce power demand from Utilities.

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Not much joy here - as of the 5/15/25 close, BUI was selling at a 1.17% premium to its NAV/share, vs. its 1- and 3-year discounts of 1.23% and 1.85%, respectively, and its flat par 5-year average pricing.

Parting Thoughts:

While BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust's defensive characteristics are attractive, we prefer to wait for a discount to NAV before buying shares, rather than buying it at its current premium to NAV.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.
