data.title: AT&T Inc.: A Secure Income Play With Series C Preferred Shares
data.description:
AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Depositary Shares, Series C (NYSE:T.PR.C), offer investors a fixed-income-like security supported by one of the most important telecommunications companies in the US. AT&T (T) is by itself a blue-chip telecom in the US, and its fundamentals have steadily improved over time, in particular its stable and relatively predictable free cash flows. In that sense, T.PR.C is T’s series C preferred shares, which are cumulative with optional redemption rights beginning in 2025. In my view, these preferreds look like a reasonably safe alternative for income investors even during times of economic uncertainty.

Telecom Bluechip Preferreds

AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Series C is actually an ADS. These represent fractional interests in its 4.75% of the Perpetual Preferred Stock backed by one of the largest telecommunications companies in the US. Each depositary share corresponds to 1/1,000th of a preferred share with a $1,000 stated value. And as you might expect, a regular preferred share might be too much of a capital commitment for some investors, so that’s why the ADS under T.PR.C is useful.

The key features of this Series C preferred are a fixed annual dividend of $1.1875 per depositary share, equivalent to 4.75% of par on February 1, May 1, August 1, and November 1. Also, its dividends are cumulative. This means any unpaid dividends accrue until paid, although historically, AT&T has always made its payments on time so far.

Additionally, these preferred shares are redeemable, in whole or in part, at the option of AT&T beginning February 18, 2025. The redemption price is $25.00 per depositary share, plus any accrued and unpaid dividends. So in that sense, it’s always possible you might get your capital back earlier than you anticipate, but other than that, it’s reasonable to consider it a long-term investment. Having said that, holders of the Series C don’t have voting rights. The only scenario where they can have a vote is if AT&T misses its dividend payments for six or more quarters.

AT&T's Business Safely Supports Its Dividend

As a company, AT&T generates stable cash flows from its wireless services and fiber operations, which are the two sectors with regular revenue and high client retention. In Q1 2025, AT&T reported $30.6 billion in revenues. And in fact, they’re now in a stronger position compared to years ago, when T experienced pressure from elevated debt levels. Moreover, their free cash flow reached $3.1 billion for Q1 2025, and they’re on track for $16 billion in FCF for the full year 2025. This matters because, as a holder of the T.PR.C ADS, all you really care about is that the company has enough resources to continue supporting your dividend.

For example, AT&T’s mobile and fiber core services are growing steadily. Mobile service revenue was up 4.1% YoY, with an average revenue per user up 1.8%. Fiber segment also grew, with 261,000 net fiber adds, and revenue up 19% YoY. These are high-margin businesses that can sustain dividend payments even if the economy faces uncertainty. It’s hard to imagine that there will be a sharp decline in demand for these types of services even during a serious economic recession.

Having said that, AT&T has to first serve its debt obligations. It’s only after they’ve made all their debt and interest payments that the preferred dividends can be paid. At the bottom of the FCF seniority is the regular common stock dividends. AT&T has historically maintained a good credit rating, honoring its capital commitments even in financial stress periods.

In any event, over the years, we’ve seen AT&T’s focus shift towards its core telecommunications business. In the process, they’ve shed non-core assets and balanced their sheet while improving margins. For example, if we go back to 2020, they reported a full-year net margin of -3.62%. But if we look at their TTM Q1 2025 figures, their profit margins have already recovered markedly to 9.64%.

Similarly, their Q4 2020 debt reached $155.2 billion (excluding other regular operating liabilities). But by Q1 2025, their debt had reduced significantly to just $127.9 billion. In fact, their net debt was reduced by $1 billion in Q1 2025 alone. And interestingly, their net debt has declined by approximately $32 billion since 2020. Of course, in the process, they cut down on other non-core assets, but strategic areas like Fiber and 5G connectivity remain their priorities. After all, these are critical infrastructure services that are less sensitive to economic downturns compared to discretionary sectors.

So overall, it’s not just a healthier balance sheet, but also a safer set of cash flows because they’re now doubling down on the most resilient assets they had. In my view, all of this suggests that going forward, their revenues are very unlikely to have significant sudden contractions, which is vital for the continued support of preferred dividends regardless of the economic cycle.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, the Series C math is rather straightforward. First, from what I can tell, it has the same seniority as the rest of AT&T’s preferreds. So, in that sense, we can simply look at the whole amount it spends on interest and preferred dividends to gauge how safe the dividend is. We also know that there are currently 48.0 million Series A preferred shares outstanding (T.PR.A) and 70.0 million Series C shares outstanding. The Series B has been retired, so it’s no longer an issue to consider.

In total, each Series A pays a $0.3125 quarterly dividend per ADS. And the Series C pays $0.296875 quarterly per ADS. That means AT&T needs to pay roughly $35.8 million in quarterly preferred dividends. On top of that, AT&T’s latest Q1 report showed they generated $9.1 billion in cash flows from operations and spent $4.3 billion in CAPEX. And just to be extra safe, we can also consider their $1.7 billion in interest expenses for Q1 2025. In total, their CFOs seem to cover easily both the CAPEX and interest expenses, and have $3.1 left for dividends and their operations.

And by comparison, the $35.8 million required for their preferred dividends seems more than safe, which is why I believe this is probably one of the safest income plays out there besides US treasuries. Also, the Series C now trades at a $18.96 PPS, and yields $1.1875 in yearly dividends, implying a respectable preferred yield of 6.26%. This is also in line with the Series A preferreds, which trade at $19.91 and yield 6.28%.

In other words, both series are basically the same in terms of yield. And if you compare these yields to the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.42%, it actually seems you get a lot of bang for your buck. The Series C basically gives you 41.6% more yield than the risk-free rate, and in my view, the extra risk here is relatively negligible given how reliable and safe AT&T’s underlying business is. This is why I think the recent pullback in the Series C is probably a decent entry price for new investors seeking a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.

Having said that, during AT&T’s latest earnings call, management acknowledged some tariff concerns, especially on smartphones and network equipment. The firm expects a minor impact that can be overcome by adjusting pricing, offering longer payment plans, or higher service fees. Besides, the company considers that its supply chain is diversified enough because AT&T acquires its equipment, devices, and materials from different suppliers and countries. So, while this macro risk could become an issue, it still seems mostly insulated from the ongoing trade concerns.

Similarly, AT&T does have a share buyback program that should start in Q2 2025. AT&T is targeting up to $3 billion in repurchases by the end of 2025. Plus, the board of directors of AT&T has approved a plan to buy back up to $10 billion of the company’s shares over time. So this program could potentially divert some of AT&T’s funds towards common investors and may weaken its balance sheet a bit more than ideal for Preferred shares investors.

Likewise, I see the trading volume for these Series C preferreds is just about 114.2 thousand shares. At the current $18.6 PPS, it implies a somewhat shallow trading liquidity of $2.2 million. So, even relatively small buy or sell orders can push the price, and would probably incur noticeable slippage for larger investors. But still, since this ticker is publicly traded on the NYSE, it will always be easy to find buyers or sellers, which makes it a highly liquid preferred compared to other lesser-known preferreds not listed on exchanges or in private markets.

Conclusion: Worth A “Buy” For Income Investors

Overall, I’m impressed by how compelling these AT&T preferences are at these levels. Naturally, it’s a relatively low yield compared to other riskier pure equity plays out there. But on the flip side, these are one of the safest dividends in the market in my view. And given their healthy economic situation, management is focused on shareholder returns, and the long-term reliability of the Series C dividends appears strong for investors seeking stable income from a blue-chip issuer. Thus, I think the recent pullback on these ADS is probably a good buying opportunity for income investors who want a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.
